coredata.dc:title: Pd nanoparticles immobilized on magnetic carbon dots@Fe3O4 nanocubes as a synergistic catalyst for hydrogen generation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Yali
Qian, Jing
Iqbal, Anam
Zhang, Lilan
Liu, Weisheng
Qin, Wenwu
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a cube-like Pd/carbon dots@Fe3O4 (Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4) hybrid material has been successfully prepared through a facile ultrasonic assisted chemical reduction method, and used as a highly efficient catalyst for the hydrolytic dehydrogenation of NaBH4 in alkaline media. It is found that the small Pd nanoparticles (NPs) are uniform and well dispersed on the surface of C-dots@Fe3O4 nanocubes (NCs). Benefiting from the advantages of the unique cube-like structure, the super conductivity of carbon dots (C-dots) and the synergistic effect between Pd NPs and C-dots@Fe3O4 support, Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs exhibits the highest catalytic performance among all the as-prepared samples. The possible reaction mechanism is discussed. Furthermore, the effects of reaction temperature, NaBH4 concentration and NaOH concentration on the catalytic activity of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs are studied. Besides, the magnetic properties of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs can achieve effective momentum transfer with the assistance of the external magnetic field, and a higher catalytic activity is observed for Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs in self-stirring mode than in magnetic-stirring mode. This novel catalyst also exhibits good stability and can be easily separated by a magnet, showing great potential for renewable energy applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanocubes
Catalytic
Magnetic momentum transfer
Hydrogen generation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Catalytic activity of the Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs
Catalytic performance and magnetic momentum transfer properties of the Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs
Effects of temperature for the catalytic performance
Effects of NaBH4 and NaOH concentration for the catalytic performance
Potential mechanism for the catalytic reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) C-dots, (b) Fe3O4 NCs and (c, d) C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs.

TEM images of (a, b and c) Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs with different magnifications. (d) HRTEM image of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs. (e) EDX pattern of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs (copper grid). (f) High-angle annular dark-field TEM image and mapping results of the elements C, N, Fe, and Pd for the boxed area.
XRD patterns of (a) C-dots, (b) Fe3O4, (c) C-dots@Fe3O4 and (d) Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs. High resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) Fe 2p and (e) Pd 3d for the Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs.
Magnetic hysteresis loops of Fe3O4, C-dots@Fe3O4 and Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs. The inset pattern shows the magnetic separation of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs from aqueous solution.

(a) Hydrogen evolution curves of the hydrolysis of alkaline NaBH4 solution (2 wt% NaBH4, 0.2 wt% NaOH, reaction temperature: 30 °C) in the absence and presence of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs catalyst under various stirring modes. (b) Hydrogen evolution curves of the hydrolysis of alkaline NaBH4 solution catalyzed by different as-prepared catalysts.
Hydrogen generation rate influenced by temperature in the presence of the (a) Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs and (b) bare Pd NPs catalysts. (c) The corresponding Arrhenius plots of Ink vs. 1/T.

(a) The reusability of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs catalyst for the hydrolysis of NaBH4. (b) TEM image of the Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs after 5 cycles. (c) XRD patterns of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs before and after the reaction.
Proposed mechanism for the catalytic hydrolysis of NaBH4 by Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs.

head.ce:title: Pd nanoparticles immobilized on magnetic carbon dots@Fe3O4 nanocubes as a synergistic catalyst for hydrogen generation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province
State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a cube-like Pd/carbon dots@Fe3O4 (Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4) hybrid material has been successfully prepared through a facile ultrasonic assisted chemical reduction method, and used as a highly efficient catalyst for the hydrolytic dehydrogenation of NaBH4 in alkaline media. It is found that the small Pd nanoparticles (NPs) are uniform and well dispersed on the surface of C-dots@Fe3O4 nanocubes (NCs). Benefiting from the advantages of the unique cube-like structure, the super conductivity of carbon dots (C-dots) and the synergistic effect between Pd NPs and C-dots@Fe3O4 support, Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs exhibits the highest catalytic performance among all the as-prepared samples. The possible reaction mechanism is discussed. Furthermore, the effects of reaction temperature, NaBH4 concentration and NaOH concentration on the catalytic activity of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs are studied. Besides, the magnetic properties of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs can achieve effective momentum transfer with the assistance of the external magnetic field, and a higher catalytic activity is observed for Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs in self-stirring mode than in magnetic-stirring mode. This novel catalyst also exhibits good stability and can be easily separated by a magnet, showing great potential for renewable energy applications.

ce:section.ce:para: With the aggravation of world energy crisis, the development of clean, sustainable energy resources has attracted increasing attentions [1]. Hydrogen is considered to be one of the most promising candidates for future energy, owing to its high energy density and abundant sources [2–6]. The search for safe and efficient hydrogen storage materials is still a big challenge for establishing a hydrogen-based energy system [7]. After decades of efforts, various hydrogen storage materials have been considered, including alcohols, formic acid, hydrazine, ammonia borane and chemical hybrid systems [8–10]. Among various storage materials, sodium borohydride (NaBH4) has been extensively investigated and regarded as a promising chemical hydrogen storage material due to its high hydrogen storage capacity (10.8 wt%), low molecular weight (37.83 g mol−1), nontoxicity, good stability in alkaline solutions, and possible regeneration capacity [11–13]. Typically, hydrogen stored in NaBH4 can be released through thermolysis [14] and hydrolysis [15–19], among which catalytic hydrolysis of NaBH4 seems to be the most effective route and generally gives a fast rate for hydrogen release under mild reaction conditions.

To date, a variety of catalysts, such as metal nanoparticles (NPs) [20], metal oxides [21,22], and multicomponent metallic systems [23–25] have been demonstrated to accelerate the hydrolysis reaction. However, owing to high surface energy, these catalysts usually suffered serious aggregation during the reaction, leading to a significant loss of catalytic activity [26]. To solve this problem, various support materials such as carbon black [27], graphene oxide [28,29], silica [30], and polymer [31,32] have been used for immobilizing the bare nanomaterials to improve their stability.

Carbon dots (C-dots), as a new class of carbon nanomaterials, have received much attention because of their excellent photostability, favorable biocompatibility, low toxicity, and super conductivity [33–36]. The abundant functional groups on the surface of C-dots give them as an excellent support for the stabilization of catalyst and the enhanced catalytic activities of the original materials [37]. So far, some groups have reported metal (Au [38], Ag [39], Pd [40], Cu [41], and AuAg alloys [42]) NPs@C-dots hybrid materials. Although the use of C-dots as support material can effectively disperse the metal NPs, however, the separation of such catalysts from the reaction medium is difficult during the filtration or centrifugation process. Therefore, the development of new catalysts with magnetically separable capacity is highly desirable.

In the present work, combining the merits of C-dots, active metal NPs, nanostructures and magnetic properties, we design a facile ultrasonic assisted chemical reduction method to fabricate Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 to form a cube-like structure. The as-obtained Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs are characterized and employed as a catalyst for hydrogen generation from NaBH4 solution, and the relationship between the structure and the catalytic activities is investigated. The magnetic momentum transfer properties of the catalyst and the possible reaction mechanism are also discussed. Furthermore, the effects of reaction temperature, NaBH4 concentration, NaOH concentration, and stability of catalyst for the hydrolysis of NaBH4 have been systematically studied.

All the chemicals used in the experiments were analytical grade and were used without further purification. FeSO4·7H2O (analytical grade), NaOH (≥96%) and KNO3 (≥99%) were obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Reagent Company. NaBH4 (≥96%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chem. Reagent Co., Ltd. Palladium chloride (PdCl2) was supplied from Energy Chemical. o-Phenylenediamine (o-PD, ≥98%) was bought from Mclean reagent company.
Synthesis of palladium nanoparticles immobilized on C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs (Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs).

C-dots, Fe3O4 NCs and C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs were prepared according to the previous reports, and the details are described in Sections 1.1–1.3 of the Supporting Information [43–45]. In a typical procedure, 1 mg of the prepared C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs was dispersed in ultrapure water (10 mL) by ultrasonication. Then, 1 mL of PdCl2 (1 mM) aqueous solution was slowly added into the above solution by ultrasonic agitation for 30 min. Subsequently, 0.25 mL of freshly prepared NaBH4 (0.1 mM) solution was added dropwise with constant ultrasonic agitation. After aging for another 30 min, the product was washed with water and dissolved as an aqueous solution (1 mg mL−1) to generate a colloidal suspension of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs. The bare Pd NPs was also synthesized under the same conditions without the addition of C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic activity of the Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic activity of the Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs towards the hydrolysis of NaBH4 was determined by measuring the rate of hydrogen (H2) generation in a typical water-filled gas burette system [24]. Typically, a two-necked round-bottom flask containing the catalyst suspension (1 mL, 1 mg mL−1) was placed on a magnetic stirrer and connected to a water-filled inverted burette. Then 5 mL of alkaline NaBH4 solution (2 wt% NaBH4, 0.2 wt% NaOH) was injected through a syringe. The temperature was maintained at 303 K, unless otherwise stated. The volume of the evolved gas was monitored by recording the displacement of water in the gas burette. In magnetic-stirring mode, a magneton was employed in the flask. In self-stirring mode, only the catalyst and the reaction mixture were loaded into the flask. The stirring rate was fixed at 1000 rpm. In addition, the control experiments were also carried out in a similar way by using other different as-prepared catalysts.
To study the reusability of the catalyst, the catalyst was recovered by using a magnet and then washed with water and ethanol several times for the next cycle.

The morphologies of C-dots, Fe3O4, C-dots@Fe3O4 and Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs are investigated by TEM. Fig. 1 a indicates that the as-prepared C-dots are mostly spherical dots that are well separated from each other, with an average size of about 0.97 nm. Fig. 1b shows TEM image of the Fe3O4 NCs with cubic-like nanostructures. Typical TEM images of the obtained C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs are shown in Fig. 1c–d. It can be seen that an ultrathin continuous C-dots layer covers the Fe3O4 NCs surface. With the aid of ultrasonic waves, the C-dots will spontaneously coat on the surface of Fe3O4 NCs to achieve a minimum surface energy [45,46]. The magnified TEM image further demonstrates that the Fe3O4 NCs are coated with ultrathin C-dot shells, which can protect the Fe3O4 NCs from being destroyed by acid media (Fig. 1d).

The Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs is prepared through the ultrasonic assisted chemical reduction method. Fig. 2 a–d presents the typical TEM images of the Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs. It can be clearly seen that the small Pd NPs are well dispersed on the surface of C-dots@Fe3O4, maintaining the shape and size of original C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs. More structural information about the Pd NPs is provided by the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 2d), from which the lattice fringe spacing of the Pd particle is measured to be 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (111) plane of Pd NPs (Fig. 2d) [47]. To evaluate the composition and elemental distribution of the hybrid material, the corresponding EDX and TEM elemental mapping analysis are performed (Fig. 2e–f). The result reveals that the elements C, N, O, Fe, and Pd are present in the hybrid material, and the Pd NPs are evenly dispersed on the surface of C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs. The C, H, Fe and Pd ratios of the Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs were obtained by elemental analysis and ICP-AES, and the values are listed in Table S1 (see the ESI†).

The formation process of the Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs is proposed as follows: when the palladium salt is mixed with the C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs suspension, the Pd ions get attached to the C-dots shell by ion exchange or coordination reactions, which can be explained by the FT-IR spectra of C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs and Pd2+-C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs (Fig. S1†). The intensity of the absorption peaks becomes obviously weaker than that of C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs, suggesting that there are coordination interactions between the functional groups on the surface of C-dots shell and Pd ions [40]. After the addition of a reducing reagent, the Pd ions are reduced in situ to Pd atoms. We have collected the residual solution at different reaction times by removing the solid intermediate product and analyzed the remaining concentrations of Pd ions by using ICP-AES (Fig. S2†). It shows that the concentration of Pd ions decreases rapidly at initial stage, which indicates that the Pd ions are in situ reduced by the reducing reagent. The formation of Pd (0) species can be observed from the UV–vis absorption spectra, as shown in Fig. S3†. The absorption band at 410 nm is disappeared after the reaction, indicating the conversion of Pd (II) to Pd (0) species [48,49]. With a continuous supply of Pd atoms from the reduction of the precursor, the nuclei gradually grow into Pd NPs [50]. Besides, the Pd/Fe3O4 NCs was also prepared under same conditions without the addition of C-dots. The TEM result shows that the Pd NPs and Fe3O4 NCs are separate from each other, which further demonstrate that the C-dots play an important role to immobilize the Pd NPs (Fig. S4†).

Fig. 3 shows the XRD patterns of the C-dots, Fe3O4, C-dots@Fe3O4 and Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs. The corresponding diffraction pattern of the C-dots shows a broad diffraction peak centered at 23°, which indicates the amorphous nature of the obtained C-dots (Fig. 3a) [51,52]. As displayed in Fig. 3b, the distinct characteristic peaks at 30.2°, 35.6°, 43.2°, 53.8°, 57.1° and 62.8° can be assigned to the (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440) crystal planes of cubic Fe3O4 phase (JCPDS 19-0629) [53]. After coating with the C-dots layer, the characteristic peaks of the C-dots and Fe3O4 are observed in the resulting materials, demonstrating the formation of C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs. In comparison with C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs, a new peak (marked as *) at 2θ = 40.2° is observed for the Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs, which can be indexed to the (111) crystal plane of Pd (JCPDS 46-1043) [54]. The above result suggested that the Pd NPs are grown along the (111) facet direction, which is consistent with the HRTEM result.

The surface elemental composition and chemical state of the Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs are analyzed by XPS. Fig. 4 a shows the XPS survey spectrum of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs, which reveals the coexistence of C, N, O, Fe and Pd elements in the hybrid material. The high resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 4b) is divided into four peaks centered at 284.7, 285.5, 286.3 and 288.8 eV, which are attributed to CC, CN, CO and OCO, respectively [55]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) exhibits three peaks at 399.7, 400.2 and 401.3 eV corresponding to the CNC, N(C)3 and N–H bands, respectively [56,57]. These results indicate that there are abundant functional groups on the surface of the C-dots. Moreover, the Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 4d) shows typical peaks of Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2 of oxidized Fe species [58]. On the other hand, the Pd 3d XPS spectrum displays four distinctive peaks (Fig. 4e), from which the peaks at 335.3 and 340.6 eV can be attributed to the Pd (0) 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, respectively. The peaks at 338.0 and 343.1 eV can be assigned to the Pd (II) 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, respectively, which may be caused by surface oxidation during the sample preparation process for XPS measurements [59].

Furthermore, the functional groups of the as-prepared C-dots, C-dots@Fe3O4 and Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs were identified by FTIR (Fig. S5†). In the FTIR spectrum of C-dots, there are obvious absorption bands at 3407, 1632, 1392 and 1247 cm−1, which correspond to OH, CO (amide I band), CN and CO stretching vibrations, respectively [57]. These functional groups on the surface of C-dots make it easier to coordinate with Pd2+. Compared to the FTIR spectrum of the C-dots, a new peak is observed at 578 cm−1, which can be ascribed to the stretching vibration of FeO bonds in the Fe3O4 structure [60]. The FTIR spectrum of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs is similar to that of C-dots@Fe3O4, however, the intensity of the absorption peaks becomes weaker, which can be attributed to the bonding interactions between the functional groups and metal [33,40]. The FTIR results are consistent with the XPS measurements.

Magnetization hysteresis loops of the Fe3O4, C-dots@Fe3O4 and Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs were measured at room temperature by using a vibrating sample magnetometer, as shown in Fig. 5 . The saturation magnetization of Fe3O4, C-dots@Fe3O4 and Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs are 76.0, 72.0 and 62.5 emu g−1, respectively. The values of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 are lower than those of Fe3O4, which can be attributed to the presence of C-dots and Pd NPs. The magnetic property of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 hybrid material allows their easy magnetic separation from the solution and recovery after their use. It is noteworthy that the magnetic properties of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs endows it with potential application in the magnetic momentum transfer process, which is a novel technology to achieve momentum transfer in a noncontact way through external fields [61,62].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic performance and magnetic momentum transfer properties of the Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic performance and magnetic momentum transfer properties of the Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs towards the hydrolytic dehydrogenation of NaBH4 are investigated in a typical water-filled gas burette system. In the absence of the catalyst, almost no hydrogen was generated. After the addition of the Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs, the hydrogen was released immediately without any induction period. Furthermore, the Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs presents higher catalytic activity in external field assisted self-stirring mode than that of in magnetic-stirring mode or static state (Fig. 6 a). When a magneton is employed in the reaction system, the Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs can be easily attracted onto the magneton surface and prevented from efficiently dispersing in the reaction mixture (Fig. S6†); hence the catalytic efficiency of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs is decreased [62]. The magnetism of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs can be employed to transfer momentum from the external magnetic field to the reaction mixture; therefore the Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs can play a dual role as both a catalyst and a stirring bar in the reaction system. For comparison, the catalytic performances of C-dots, C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs, bare Pd NPs and physically mixed Pd NPs and C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs were also investigated under similar experimental conditions (Fig. 6b). Fig. 6b shows the relationship between the volume of H2 and the reaction time for the different as-prepared catalysts. It can be seen that Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs exhibits higher catalytic activities than bare Pd NPs (Fig. S7†) and other samples. The improved catalytic activity of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs can be attributed to the specific characteristics of its nanostructure and the synergistic effect between Pd NPs and C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs [63]. In contrast, the rapid aggregation of bare Pd NPs during the catalytic reaction generally results in a decrease of the catalytic activities [64]. The above results demonstrate that the rational design of the Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs catalyst can be an effective approach for enhancing its catalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of temperature for the catalytic performance
ce:section.ce:para: To study the effects of temperature for the catalytic performance of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs and bare Pd NPs, hydrolytic reactions were carried out at different temperatures ranging from 303 K to 333 K. As shown in Fig. 7 a–b, the H2 generation rate (k) increases with the rise of temperature, which suggested that a high temperature is beneficial for enhancing the dehydrogenation rate of NaBH4.

The apparent activation energy (Ea) is an important parameter for a catalytic reaction, which can be utilized to assess the catalytic activity of the catalyst [15]. According to the Arrhenius equation (Eq. (1)) and the linear relationship between Ink and 1/T (Eq. (2), Fig. 7c), the Ea of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs and bare Pd NPs for the catalytic hydrolysis of NaBH4 are calculated to be 46.72 kJ mol−1 and 61.65 kJ mol−1, respectively. This result indicates that the combination of Pd NPs and C-dots@Fe3O4 support greatly reduces the activation energy, which facilitates the reaction at lower temperatures. The Ea value for Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs is also lower than the previously reported results (Table S2, see the ESI†) [16–20,23,25].
(1)
k =
Ae
−
E a
/RT
(2)
ln
k = ln
A
−
E a
/RT

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of NaBH4 and NaOH concentration for the catalytic performance
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of the NaBH4 concentration for the rate of H2 generation is also investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. S8a†. Briefly, the H2 generation rate rises remarkably with an increase of the NaBH4 concentration from 1 wt% to 5 wt%, and then gradually decreases as the concentration of NaBH4 reached to 10 wt%, indicating that the excessive NaBH4 is unfavourable for the H2 evolution. This phenomenon may be ascribed to the presence of excessive NaBH4 which can lead to an increase in the solution viscosity and inevitably retards the mass transfer process, meanwhile, reducing the hydrolysis efficiency [24]. Fig. S8b† shows the effect of NaOH concentrations on hydrogen generation rates. When the NaOH concentration increases up to 0.2 wt%, the hydrogen generation rate is accelerated. However, further increasing the NaOH concentration to 1.0 wt%, the rates of hydrogen production have been decreased. It is well known that excessive NaOH can lead to a decrease in the solubility of the NaBO2 formed in the hydrolysis reaction, which deposits on the surface of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs, and decreases the H2 generation rate [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential mechanism for the catalytic reaction
ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of the experimental results and the reported literatures [65–67], we proposed a possible pathway for hydrogen generation from NaBH4 catalyzed by Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs, which is shown in Scheme 1 . It suggested that there are interactions between the NaBH4 molecule and the Pd NPs to produce the activated intermediate species (M–BH3 − and M−H), which can be attacked by H2O molecule to generate the BH3(OH)− ion and H2. The cycle of charge transfer continues as BH3(OH)− → BH2(OH)2 − → BH(OH)3 − → B(OH)4 −, releasing H2 at each step. The superior catalytic performance of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs can be ascribed to the high surface activity of the Pd NPs and the synergistic effect between Pd NPs and C-dots@Fe3O4 support. The support materials of C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs not only stabilized the Pd NPs, but also increased the surface area and promoted the catalytic activity. In addition, the good electron-transport properties of the C-dots shell [35,36] facilitate the electron transfer (ET) and further enhance the catalytic rate.

For the heterogeneous catalyst, high stability and favorable cycle performance should be taken into account. Herein, the Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs can be easily separated from the reaction mixture by using a magnet and reused for the next cycle. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the activity of the as-prepared Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs catalyst has no significant decreases after five successive cycles, which indicates its favorable cycle performance in the hydrolysis of NaBH4. The morphology and structure of the used Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs were analyzed by TEM and XRD, and the results are shown in Fig. 8b–c. The analytical results reveal that the catalyst could well maintain its morphology and structure, suggesting that the C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs can stabilize the Pd NPs during the reaction.

In summary, Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs has been successfully prepared by using an easy and general ultrasonic assisted chemical reduction method. The Pd NPs is effectively immobilized on the surface of C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs, which is confirmed by TEM, EDX, XRD, FTIR and XPS analyses. The catalytic performance and magnetic momentum transfer properties of the Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs towards hydrolysis of NaBH4 were investigated. It is noted that the Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs shows the highest catalytic activities among all the as-prepared catalysts, which can be attributed to its unique cube-like structure and the synergistic effect between Pd NPs and C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs support. The C-dots shell plays an important role to protect the Fe3O4 NCs and immobilize the Pd NPs, and further enhance the catalytic rate owing to its good electron-transport properties. The magnetic property of Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs endows it with potential application in the magnetic momentum transfer process.

Moreover, the activation energy for the Pd/C-dots@Fe3O4 NCs is measured to be about 46.72 kJ mol−1, which is lower than that of most reported catalysts. Furthermore, the catalyst is reused for five times without the significant loss of its catalytic activity. These findings may provide a way for the concise preparation of high performance and durability nano-hybrid catalysts for renewable energy applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation for Fostering Talents in Basic Research of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. J1103307). The authors would like to thank the Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 21271094).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2017.04.253.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High capacity hydrogen storage: basic aspects, new developments and milestones
Manganese compounds as water oxidizing catalysts for hydrogen production via water splitting: from manganese complexes to nano-sized manganese oxides
Polypeptide and Mn-Ca oxide: toward a biomimetic catalyst for water-splitting systems
Toward a nanosized iron based water-oxidizing catalyst
Base metal catalyzed dehydrogenation of Ammonia−Borane for chemical hydrogen storage
Hydrogen generation from formic acid and alcohols using homogeneous catalysts
Gold supported on zirconia polymorphs for hydrogen generation from formic acid in base-free aqueous medium
Ag/Pd nanoparticles supported on amine-functionalized metal–organic framework for catalytic hydrolysis of ammonia borane
Sodium borohydride as a fuel for the future
Hydrogen generation from alkaline NaBH4 solution using Co-Ni-Mo-P/g-Al2O3 catalysts
Fast hydrogen generation from NaBH4 hydrolysis catalyzed by nanostructured Co-Ni-B catalysts
CoO nanocrystals as a highly active catalyst for the generation of hydrogen from hydrolysis of sodium borohydride
Cobalt–nickel–phosphorus supported on Pd-activated TiO2 (Co–Ni–P/Pd-TiO2) as cost-effective and reusable catalyst for hydrogen generation from hydrolysis of alkaline sodium borohydride solution
